subject: Chemistry
title: Uranium: From Paint to Power
description: A 1-minute de-risking session on Uranium: From Paint to Power.

hook.text: Before it powered reactors, uranium was used to make bright orange glaze for dinner plates.

buildup.text: Martin Klaproth discovered uranium in 1789 and named it after the planet Uranus, discovered eight years earlier.

discovery.text: Henri Becquerel found in 1896 that uranium emits invisible rays—the discovery of radioactivity.

twist.text: For decades, uranium was a curiosity. Then Hahn and Strassmann split its nucleus in 1938—nuclear fission.

climax.text: Uranium now fuels power plants generating 10% of the world's electricity. Same element, new purpose.

punchline.text: Orange plates to nuclear power. Uranium found its real purpose.

quiz.question: What was uranium used for before nuclear energy?
options: As a colorant for ceramic glazes and glass
